title: Stripe MRR vs Accounting Software: Why They Disagree
meta_description: Your Stripe dashboard says $12,400 MRR. QuickBooks says $9,800. Here's why they disagree and which number you should actually trust.
h1: Why Your Stripe Dashboard Shows a Different MRR Than Your Accounting Software (And Which One to Trust)
body_markdown:
You open Stripe. It says $12,400 MRR. You open QuickBooks (or Xero, or whatever your accountant made you use). It says $9,800 in monthly recurring revenue. Your investor update goes out in four hours.

Which number is right?

If you've ever had this mini panic, you're not alone. The gap between Stripe's MRR and what your accounting software reports is one of the most common sources of confusion for early-stage SaaS founders. And it's almost never because one system is broken. They're just measuring different things, in different ways, on different timelines.

Here's what's actually happening and how to decide which number belongs on your pitch deck versus your tax return.

## The Short Answer

Stripe shows you **normalized recurring revenue** based on active subscriptions. Your accounting software shows you **recognized revenue** based on GAAP (or cash, depending on your setup). These are supposed to be different numbers. The problem is that nobody tells you that when you sign up for either tool.

For running your business day-to-day, trust Stripe's MRR (with some adjustments we'll cover). For filing taxes, talking to investors who care about audits, or selling your company, trust your accounting software.

Now let's get into why they diverge.

## Reason 1: Stripe Annualizes, Accounting Software Doesn't

This is the biggest source of confusion.

When a customer pays you $1,200 upfront for an annual plan, Stripe's dashboard divides that by 12 and adds $100 to your MRR. Makes sense—it's "monthly recurring revenue," so annual plans get normalized to a monthly number.

Your accounting software, if it's set up correctly on accrual basis, does something different. It books the full $1,200 as deferred revenue on the balance sheet and recognizes $100 per month over the following 12 months as actual revenue.

If it's set up on cash basis (common for bootstrapped founders under $1M ARR), it books the full $1,200 as revenue the day it hits your bank account—then nothing for the next 11 months.

So in a month where you sold three annual plans:
- Stripe MRR: +$300
- Cash-basis accounting: +$3,600 this month, $0 for 11 months
- Accrual accounting: +$300 this month, +$300 for 11 months

None of these are wrong. They answer different questions.

## Reason 2: Failed Payments and Involuntary Churn

Stripe's MRR number typically includes subscriptions that are in a "past due" state. The subscription is technically still active, retry logic is running, and Stripe assumes the payment will eventually go through.

Your accounting software only sees money that actually moved. If a $99/month customer's card failed on the 3rd and didn't recover until the 18th, your accounting software shows $0 from them until the 18th. Stripe's MRR dashboard still counted them the whole time.

At any given moment, 5-8% of a typical SaaS's subscriptions are in some kind of failed-payment state. If you're at $20k MRR by Stripe's count, that's potentially $1,000-$1,600 in "revenue" that hasn't actually been collected and may never be.

## Reason 3: Refunds, Disputes, and Chargebacks

Stripe's default MRR calculation doesn't subtract refunds the way you'd expect. A customer who paid $500 and got fully refunded last week might still show up in your "new business" metrics for the month they signed up.

Accounting software, in contrast, tracks every refund as a contra-revenue entry and reduces your recognized revenue accordingly. Chargebacks are even more brutal—by the time a chargeback lands, your accounting software has often already recognized the revenue and now has to reverse it, plus book a $15 dispute fee.

This is why your bookkeeper's revenue number is almost always lower than Stripe's headline number.

## Reason 4: Stripe Fees

Your Stripe MRR is gross. Your accounting software revenue might be gross or net, depending on how your bookkeeper set it up.

A $100 Visa transaction nets you about $97.10 after Stripe's 2.9% + $0.30 fee. Some accounting setups record $100 as revenue and $2.90 as a processing expense. Others just record $97.10 as revenue. Both are acceptable under GAAP, but they produce different-looking P&Ls.

If your accountant is using the "net" method, your accounting revenue will be roughly 3% lower than Stripe's number just from fees alone.

## Reason 5: Proration, Upgrades, and Downgrades

When a customer upgrades mid-cycle from $50 to $150, Stripe prorates the difference and charges them immediately for the remaining days. Your MRR jumps by $100.

Accounting software handles this a dozen different ways depending on how the integration was configured. Sometimes it books the proration as one-time revenue. Sometimes it splits it across the remaining period. Sometimes it ignores it entirely until the next full billing cycle.

Multiply this by a couple dozen upgrade, downgrade, add-seat, and remove-seat events per month, and you've got a meaningful divergence by quarter-end.

## Reason 6: Timing of Sync

Most Stripe-to-accounting integrations run on a schedule—hourly, daily, or when a specific webhook fires. If you're looking at Stripe live and your accounting software synced 18 hours ago, you're comparing two different moments in time. For a business processing dozens of transactions per day, that alone can explain a few hundred dollars of discrepancy.

## So Which Number Should You Trust?

It depends on the decision you're making.

**Trust Stripe's MRR (adjusted) when you're:**
- Tracking growth trends week-over-week
- Deciding whether to hire or cut spend
- Reporting to early-stage investors who care about momentum
- Running pricing experiments
- Calculating LTV and CAC payback

**Trust your accounting software when you're:**
- Filing taxes
- Preparing for an acquisition or due diligence
- Talking to a bank about a loan
- Presenting to investors who will audit
- Reconciling at year-end

The worst thing you can do is quote Stripe's raw MRR to an acquirer. They will tie out every dollar to your bank statements, and any discrepancy becomes a reason to lower the multiple.

## The "Adjusted" Part Matters

When I said "trust Stripe MRR adjusted," here's what adjusted means:

1. Subtract subscriptions in past-due state for more than 14 days
2. Subtract any customer who refunded within the last 30 days
3. Don't count one-time charges, setup fees, or usage overages as MRR
4. Handle annual plans consistently (either always normalize or always exclude)

Stripe's native dashboard does some of this but not all. That's the gap where most founders get tripped up—they glance at the big number on the Stripe home screen, put it in their investor update, and then a month later their bookkeeper's spreadsheet tells a different story.

## Building a Single Source of Truth

The practical fix is to pick one number, document how you calculate it, and use it consistently. Change the definition and you break every historical comparison you've ever made.

For most founders under $1M ARR, that means:
- **Operational number:** Adjusted Stripe MRR, updated daily
- **Financial number:** Accrual-basis recognized revenue from your accounting software, updated monthly
- **Reconciliation:** A spreadsheet or tool that maps one to the other, so you can explain the gap when someone asks

If you're doing this in a spreadsheet and finding yourself exporting Stripe CSVs every week to rebuild the same view, tools like [Stripe Revenue Tracker](#) pull the adjusted MRR directly from your Stripe data and show it without requiring you to log into Stripe or wait for a QuickBooks sync. It won't replace your accounting software—nothing should—but it does close the gap between "the number Stripe shows" and "the number you can actually defend."

## The Real Lesson

The discrepancy between Stripe and your accounting software isn't a bug. It's a feature of having two systems that were built for two different jobs. Once you understand what each one is measuring, you stop panicking when they disagree and start using them for what they're good at.

Your Stripe dashboard tells you how the business is running today. Your accounting software tells you what actually happened last month. You need both. You just shouldn't confuse them for each other.
